content.full_text:
KARL KETNER
==============================================================================

DATABASE MANAGEMENT
& CUSTOM PROGRAMMING
1051 Broadway
San Francisco, CA 94133
415/885-5822 (Direct/FAX)

Sep 11 2 06 PM 91

September 9, 1991


FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814

To Whom It May Concern,

I maintain political finance databases and produce disclosure
reports for a number of clients, both institutional (PACs) and
non-institutional (office-holders/candidates). I would greatly
appreciate your comments and advice on the following situation
which has arisen for clients who are candidates in the City and
County of San Francisco.

San Francisco City Ordinance provides for campaign contribution
limitations in the amount of $500 (cumulative) per individual per
election ($750 cumulative in the event of a runoff). To date, it
has been our policy to apply FPPC guidelines in defining an
individual and any business entities that individual controls as
one entity for purposes of contribution limits. For example, a
personal check from John Doe and a business check from John Doe
Manufacturing (where John Doe is the sole proprietor/owner of the
business) have been treated as two contributions from the same
single entity in determining whether the cumulative contribution
total from John Doe/John Doe Manufacturing is within allowable
contribution limits.

However, a client was recently advised by the San Francisco City
Attorney's Office that the San Francisco Ordinance allows the
contributions described in the previous example to be treated as
coming from two unique entities -- John Doe, individual; and John
Doe Manufacturing, a business -- for purposes of contribution
limits. According to the San Francisco City Attorney's Office,
both John Doe and John Doe Manufacturing may each give up to $500
cumulative per election.

Since state-level contribution limits are no longer in effect
(except for special elections), it would seem that FPPC
regulations regarding the treatment of individuals and businesses
controlled by the individual would not pre-empt the San Francisco
definition for purposes of contribution limits in effect for San
Francisco. For purposes of disclosure, I assume that FPPC

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
09/09/91

guidelines remain applicable. John Doe and John Doe
Manufacturing must be disclosed on form 490 as a single entity,
with the cumulative calendar total reflecting the aggregate
contributions from both the individual and business.

Does the San Francisco City Attorney have authority to define
separate entities differently than the FPPC for purposes of
enforcing the Political Reform Act; and, if so, do the FPPC
definitions remain applicable for purposes of campaign disclosure
reporting?

I greatly appreciate you taking the time to review this enquiry
and await your guidance.

Sincerely,

[signature]

KARL KETNER

KK:abm

sections.question: Does the san francisco City Attorney have authority to define separate entities differently than the FPPC for purposes of enforcing the Political Reform Act, and, if so, do the FPPC definitions remain applicable for purposes of campaign disclosure reporting?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether San Francisco City Attorney can define individuals and their controlled businesses as separate entities for contribution limit purposes under local ordinance, and whether FPPC entity definitions still apply for disclosure reporting.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a consultant, not an FPPC response/advice letter. The document contains the question section but no conclusion/answer from FPPC. Document is dated September 9, 1991 and addresses contribution limit interpretation for San Francisco local elections.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Does the san francisco City Attorney have authority to define separate entities differently than the FPPC for purposes of enforcing the Political Reform Act, and, if so, do the FPPC definitions remain applicable for purposes of campaign disclosure reporting?

embedding.summary: Karl Ketner, who maintains political finance databases for candidates and PACs, seeks FPPC guidance on whether San Francisco's local contribution limit ordinance can apply different entity definitions than FPPC guidelines, allowing individuals and their controlled businesses to each contribute up to $500 separately rather than being treated as a single entity.
